title: Get started with affectOU
text: univariate affective process, stochastic differential equation OU : \[d{X}(t) = {\theta}({\mu} - {X}(t))dt + {\gamma} \, d{W}(t)\] , parameters represent: \(\mu\) (mu): Attractor location, baseline “set point” affect; \(\theta\) (theta): Attractor strength, quickly affect returns baseline; \(\gamma\) (gamma): Diffusion coefficient, scale random perturbations. diffusion coefficient \(\gamma\) easily interpreted terms noise variance \(\sigma\) (sigma), \(\sigma = \gamma^2\). can create simple one-dimensional model default parameters calling affectOU constructor: Alternatively, can specify custom parameters represent, example, process stronger mean-reversion (\(\theta = 1\)), positive baseline (\(\mu = 1\)), smaller amount random perturbations (\(\sigma = 0.25\)):

title: Get started with affectOU
text: OU framework extends naturally multiple affect dimensions (e.g., valence arousal, positive negative affect). d-dimensional process, OU looks like: \[d\mathbf{X}(t) = \mathbf{\Theta}(\mathbf{\mu} - \mathbf{X}(t))dt + \mathbf{\Gamma} \, d\mathbf{W}(t)\] multivariate models, noise covariance matrix \(\Sigma\) defined \(\Sigma = \Gamma \Gamma^\top\). default, affectOU() creates model uncoupled dynamics (diagonal \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\)) identical parameters across dimensions. example, two-dimensional model, get: update() function can used modify existing model without recreating scratch: create coupled dynamics, dimensions affect time, non-diagonal \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) matrix specified. example, following creates model first dimension stronger influence second vice versa:

title: Get started with affectOU
text: can simulate data specified model simulate() function: Though OU continuous-time process, simulation requires evaluating discrete time points – either via exact sampling known transition distribution via numerical discretization methods Euler-Maruyama approximation, common numerical approach simulating stochastic differential equations. package, use latter. OU, amounts updating state \(\mathbf{X}\) discrete time-step \(\Delta t\) according following equation: \[\begin{equation} \begin{split}   \mathbf{X}_{t + \Delta t} &= \mathbf{X}_t + \mathbf{\Theta} (\mathbf{\mu} - \mathbf{X}_t) \Delta t + \mathbf{\Gamma} \mathbf{\epsilon}_t \sqrt{\Delta t} \\   \mathbf{\epsilon}_t &\sim N(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{}_d) \end{split} \end{equation}\] \(\mathbf{}_d\) \(d \times d\) identity matrix (.e., matrix 1s diagonal 0s elsewhere) \(\Delta t\) sufficiently small ensure sensible results (e.g., \(\Delta t = 0.01\)). Simulations can customized fit one’s needs, specifically changing arguments: nsim: Number replications simulations run; dt: (discretized) time step use within Euler-Maruyama approximation; stop: Total time simulate (e.g., stop = 100 simulates time 0 time 100); save_at: Time interval saved simulation values; seed: Seed use sampling random perturbations.

title: Get started with affectOU
text: first way explore simulated data plotting . affectOU provides plot function several different types plots visualize simulated data, namely:  Time series trajectory (type = "time", ou_plot_time()): Shows simulated values affect time, along horizontal line denoting baseline affective state \(\mu\). Distribution affect values (type = "histogram", ou_plot_histogram()): Shows distribution simulated affect values aggregated across time, along vertical line denoting baseline affective state \(\mu\). Autocorrelation function (type = "acf", ou_plot_acf()): Shows value autocorrelation simulated values particular lags apart, along dashed line denoting theoretical autocorrelation function implied OU model. Phase portrait (type = "phase", ou_plot_phase()): Shows affect time \(t\) relates value affect next time point \(t + \Delta t\). dashed line denotes theoretical relationship affect time \(t\) \(t + \Delta t\) implied OU model, linear relationship slope \(e^{-\mathbf{\Theta} \Delta t}\) intercept \((1 - e^{-\mathbf{\Theta} \Delta t} \mathbf{\mu})\).
code: # Time series trajectory (default) plot(sim)  # Distribution of affect values plot(sim, type = "histogram")  # Autocorrelation function with theoretical overlay plot(sim, type = "acf")  # Phase portrait (lag-1 relationship) plot(sim, type = "phase")

title: Affect characteristics implied by the OU process
text: first main feature OU model affect dynamics concerns tendency return mean. stable systems (see later sections), affect perturbed, returns toward baseline \(\mathbf{\mu}\) rate determined eigenvalues \(\mathbf{\Theta}\). unidimensional systems, \(\theta\) can simply interpreted speed emotional regulation, low \(\theta\) represents emotional inertia. can proven regulation captured matrix exponential , time step \(\Delta t\), deterministic drift system can described following equation: \[\begin{equation}   \mathbf{X}_{t + \Delta t} = (\mathbf{}_d - e^{-\mathbf{\Theta} \Delta t}) \mathbf{\mu} + e^{-\mathbf{\Theta} \Delta t} \mathbf{X}_t \end{equation}\] rate regulation determined eigenvalues \(\mathbf{\Theta}\). Decomposing: \[\begin{equation}   \mathbf{\Theta} = \mathbf{\Omega} \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{\Omega}^{-1} \end{equation}\] \(\mathbf{\Omega}\) contains eigenvectors \(\mathbf{\Lambda}\) contains eigenvalues drift matrix \(\mathbf{\Theta}\). drift can decomposed : \[\begin{equation} \begin{split}   e^{-\mathbf{\Theta} \Delta t} &= e^{-\mathbf{\Omega} \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{\Omega}^{-1} \Delta t} \\   &= \mathbf{\Omega} e^{-\mathbf{\Lambda} \Delta t} \mathbf{\Omega}^{-1} \end{split} \end{equation}\] derivation shows rate regulation determined eigenvalues \(\mathbf{\Lambda}\) directionality regulation determined eigenvectors \(\mathbf{\Omega}\). stable systems positive real parts eigenvalues, higher values \(\lambda_i \\mathbf{\Lambda}\) lead rapid regulation lower values \(\lambda_i\) indicate slower regulation. property shown , simulate two independent OU processes differing \(\theta\). process higher \(\theta\) (red) stays closer attractor (horizontal line, \(\mu\)) process lower \(\theta\) (green), strays away. emotion terms, former process displays lower affective inertia latter..
code: # For simplicity and ease of visualisation, we specify both processes in the same model.  # However, as they are uncoupled, they will evolve independently over time.  model <- affectOU(   theta = diag(c(5.0, 0.5)) )  # Simulate and plot timeseries sim <- simulate(   model,    seed = 123,    stop = 20 )  plot(   sim,   by_dim = FALSE,   main = "Mean Reversion Depends on θ",   sub = c("Fast Regulation (θ = 5.0)", "Slow Regulation (θ = 0.5)") )

title: Affect characteristics implied by the OU process
text: affect perturbed, takes time return baseline. speed return determined \(\mathbf{\Theta}\), characterizes long effect perturbations persists. rate decay \(\tau\) – sometimes referred relaxation time – can computed solution OU, specifically: \[\begin{equation}   \mathbf{y}_{\Delta t} = \mathbf{\mu} + e^{-\mathbf{\Theta} \Delta t} (\mathbf{y}_0 - \mathbf{\mu}) \end{equation}\] can define relaxation time \(\tau\) time \(\Delta t\) initial condition \(\mathbf{y}_0\) relaxed towards attractor \(\mathbf{\mu}\), retaining particular percentage original value. example, unidimensional OUs, setting relaxation time \(\tau = \theta^{-1}\) leads deviation initial condition attractor (\(\mathbf{y}_0 - \mathbf{\mu}\)) shrink \(e^{-1} \approx 0.37 = 37\%\) original value. Note set equations, can alternatively define half-life \(\tau = t_{.50} = \ln(2) \mathbf{\Theta^{-1}}\) time deviation \(\mathbf{\mu}\) shrinks 50%. relaxation time straightforward compute unidimensional OUs. derive , can use solution OU, given : \[\begin{equation}   y_{\Delta t} = \mu + e^{-\theta \Delta t} (y_0 - \mu) \end{equation}\] \(y_{\Delta t}\) represents value variable time step \(\Delta t\), \(y_0\) represents initial condition, \(\theta\) drift parameter, \(\mu\) attractor. Now, interested finding degree initial deviation attractor determines value \(y_{\Delta t}\) particular time \(\tau\) passed. words, solution, interested last part solution scaling initial deviation attractor: \[\begin{equation}   e^{-\theta \Delta t} (y_0 - \mu) \end{equation}\] denote relaxation percentage \(r \[0, 1]\), can compute corresponding relaxation time \(\tau\) follows: \[\begin{equation} \begin{split}   e^{-\theta \tau} &= r \\   -\theta \tau &= \ln(r) \\   \tau &= -\ln(r) \theta^{-1} \end{split} \end{equation}\] Exemplifying equation unidimensional OU drift coefficient \(\theta = 2\), can compute half-time \(\tau = t_{0.5}\) \(\tau = -\ln(0.5) 2^{-1} = -\frac{\ln(0.5)}{2} \approx 0.35\). logic extends multivariate OUs, assume exponential logarithm drift matrix \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) exist, assume \(d \times d\) matrix relaxation percentages \(R\) logarithm exists, corresponding relaxation times \(\tau\) can computed : \[\begin{equation} \begin{split}   e^{-\mathbf{\Theta} \tau} &= R \\   -\mathbf{\Theta} \tau &= \ln(R) \\   \tau &= -\mathbf{\Theta}^{-1} \ln(R) \end{split} \end{equation}\] Note , case, \(\tau\) \(d \times d\) matrix containing relaxation times dimension separately. One may use interpretation relaxation times cases variables captured OU independent (.e., \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) diagonal), case \(\tau\) diagonal matrix containing relaxation times per dimension separately. However, principle apply coupled multivariate OUs. Coupling variables means relaxation one variable can affect relaxation others. case, relaxation time one variable interpreted separately relaxation times variables.  ACF equals \(1/e\) relaxation time \(0.5\) half-life. Slow regulation (.e., lower \(\theta\)) means longer persistence higher autocorrelation.
code: model <- affectOU(theta = 0.5, mu = 0, sigma = 1) # Simulate with more time points for accurate ACF sim <- simulate(model, stop = 10000, dt = 0.01, save_at = 0.01) plot(sim, type = "acf", lag.max = 10)

Within affectOU(), can either specify covariance matrix \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) diffusion matrix \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\), \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\) must lower triangular matrix. noise covariance \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) intuitive parameter specify defining reactivity system, directly captures variance covariance noise across dimensions. implementation OU, noise term defined \(\mathbf{\Gamma} \, d\mathbf{W}(t)\), \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\) diffusion matrix \(d\mathbf{W}(t)\) vector independent Wiener processes. means noise covariance \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) directly specified rather derived \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\). noise covariance \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) related diffusion matrix \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\) : \[\begin{equation}   \mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{\Gamma} \mathbf{\Gamma}^T \end{equation}\] equality known Cholesky decomposition, requiring diffusion matrix \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\) lower triangular matrix. restriction ensures may convert \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\) without loss generality, meaning positive semi-definite covariance matrix \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) can represented \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\). figure shown , visualize two processes subject perturbations may show larger fluctuations based value variance \(\sigma\), higher \(\sigma\) leads greater fluctuations around attractor. emotion terms, former process emotionally reactive environmental fluctuations latter.

title: Affect characteristics implied by the OU process
text: multivariate models, -diagonal elements \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) allow coupling dimensions, meaning dynamics two variables can interlinked. example, can create coupled two-dimensional system : <!– \[\mathbf{\Theta} = \begin{bmatrix} \theta_{11} & \theta_{12} \\ \theta_{21} & \theta_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}   0.5 & 0.0 \\   -2.0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}\] following figure illustrates resulting coupled dynamics. \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) multiplied current state \(\mathbf{X}(t)\) determine deterministic drift system. example, two-dimensional system without diffusion (.e., \(\mathbf{\Gamma} = 0\)), change \(\mathbf{x}\) given : \[\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \mathbf{\Theta} (\mathbf{\mu} - \mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \theta_{11} & \theta_{12} \\ \theta_{21} & \theta_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mu_1 - x_1 \\ \mu_2 - x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \theta_{11} (\mu_1 - x_1) + \theta_{12} (\mu_2 - x_2) \\ \theta_{21} (\mu_1 - x_1) + \theta_{22} (\mu_2 - x_2) \end{bmatrix}\] change dimension \(\) determined deviation \(\mathbf{\mu}\) deviation dimension, weighted corresponding elements \(\mathbf{\Theta}{\cdot}\). means dynamics one dimension can influence dynamics dimension, creating coupled trajectories. example, shown , negative entry \(\theta_{12}\) means dimension 2 attractor, pushes dimension 1 , dimension 2 attractor, pushes dimension 1 . Interdimensional coupling can also analyzed using eigendecomposition. eigenvectors \(\mathbf{\Omega}\) shape direction along system moves towards attractor \(\mathbf{\mu}\) (case stable system). eigenvectors \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) : \[\mathbf{\Omega} \approx \begin{bmatrix}   1 & 1 \\   0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\] column \(\mathbf{\Omega}\) represents eigenvector, direction state space along system moves towards attractor. matrix defective, one linearly independent eigenvector. system effectively one-dimensional, dimensions moving together along trajectory toward attractor. behaviour similarly visible time-series plot , dimensions evolve almost identical way time.
code: # theta_12 = -2: when dimension 2 is above its attractor, it pushes dimension 1 up; # when dimension 2 is below its attractor, it pushes dimension 1 down theta_2d <- matrix(c(   0.5, -2,   0, 0.5 ), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)  # eigen(theta_2d)  model_2d <- affectOU(theta = theta_2d, mu = 0, gamma = 1) sim_2d <- simulate(model_2d) plot(sim_2d, main = "Coupled Trajectories", xlim = c(0, 20)) plot(sim_2d, type = "phase", main = "Phase Portrait")

title: Affect characteristics implied by the OU process
text: univariate case, sign \(\theta\) determines qualitative behaviour system. Three regimes can distinguished: \(\theta > 0\): Stable, mean-reverting around \(\mu\); \(\theta \approx 0\): Random walk without clear attractor, meaning process drifts without returning baseline; \(\theta < 0\): Unstable process diverges \(\mu\).
code: # For simplicity and ease of visualisation, we specify all processes in the same model. # However, as they are uncoupled, they will evolve independently over time.  model <- affectOU(theta = diag(c(0.5, 0.01, -0.3))) sim <- simulate(model, stop = 100, seed = 43) #> Warning: ! System is not stable; no stationary distribution exists. #> ℹ Defaulting `initial_state` to mu. plot(sim,   ylim = c(-10, 10), by_dim = FALSE,   main = "Affect Dynamics of Different Stability Regimes",   sub = c("θ > 0 (stable)", "θ ≈ 0 (random walk)", "θ < 0 (unstable)") )

title: Affect characteristics implied by the OU process
text: multiple dimensions, stability depends eigenvalues \(\mathbf{\Theta}\). distinguish three cases: Real positive eigenvalues: case, OU shows smooth exponential decay toward attractor \(\mathbf{\mu}\) variables; Complex eigenvalues positive real parts: case, OU display damped linear oscillations around attractor \(\mathbf{\mu}\) variables, representing cyclical affect patterns; Eigenvalue zero negative real parts: case, OU non-stationary, meaning affect diverge time – either drifting monotonically oscillating growing amplitude, depending whether eigenvalues real complex. type behaviour usually theoretical interest. , demonstrate first two cases, setting noise covariance \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) zero better show dynamics implied eigenvalues.  Note behaviour OU may also represent mix two types, combining exponential decay oscillatory patterns. example case following figure:
code: # Real eigenvalues: Smooth decay theta_real <- matrix(   c(0.2, 0.1, 0.1, 0.2),    nrow = 2 )  eigen(theta_real)$values #> [1] 0.3 0.1  # Complex eigenvalues: Oscillatory decay theta_complex <- matrix(   c(0.2, -1, 1, 0.2),    nrow = 2 )  eigen(theta_complex)$values #> [1] 0.2+1i 0.2-1i # Define the two models model_real <- affectOU(   theta = theta_real,   sigma = 0 )  model_complex <- affectOU(   theta = theta_complex,   sigma = 0 )  # Perform simulations for both systems seed <- 123 sim_real <- simulate(   model_real,   stop = 50, seed = seed,   initial_state = c(-3, 3) )  sim_complex <- simulate(   model_complex,   stop = 50, seed = seed,   initial_state = c(-3, 3) )  # Plot the resulting time-series plot(sim_real, main = "Real eigenvalues") plot(sim_complex, main = "Complex eigenvalues") # Both complex and real eigenvalues: Combination of smooth and oscillatory  # dynamics theta <- matrix(   c(0.2, 1, 0, 1, 0.2, -1, 0, 1, 0.2),    nrow = 3 )  eigen(theta)$values #> [1] 0.2000034+0.00000e+00i 0.1999983+2.93925e-06i 0.1999983-2.93925e-06i # Define the the model model <- affectOU(   theta = theta,   sigma = 0 )  # Perform simulations for the system seed <- 123 sim <- simulate(   model,   stop = 50,    seed = seed,   initial_state = c(-3, 3, 4) )  # Plot the resulting time-series plot(sim, main = "Real and complex eigenvalues")

title: Affect characteristics implied by the OU process
text: Non-zero -diagonal elements \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\) mean random fluctuations drive dimensions simultaneously — noise sources shared. can lead synchronized fluctuations even coupling deterministic part process (.e., \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) diagonal).
code: # Shared noise sources (same fluctuation drives both dims), no drift coupling gamma_2d <- matrix(c(   1, 0,   0.5, 1 ), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE) gamma_2d %*% t(gamma_2d)  # implied noise covariance Σ  model_2d <- affectOU(theta = 0.5, mu = 0, gamma = gamma_2d) sim_2d <- simulate(model_2d, seed = 105) plot(sim_2d, main = "Shared Noise Sources", xlim = c(0, 20))

title: Simulate Affect Dynamics with Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Processes
text: aim affectOU provide tools simulating Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) process R. OU widely used model affect dynamics: feelings emotions change time (e.g., Guthier et al., 2020; Kuppens et al., 2010a; Voelkle & Oud, 2013). formulates three core psychological mechanisms three sets parameters (Uhlenbeck & Ornstein, 1930; Oravecz et al., 2011): Drift matrix Θ: Governs rate affect returns baseline, capturing person’s emotion regulation capacity emotional inertia; Attractor μ: long-term average level affect system tends return , representing person’s baseline mood emotional setpoint; Diffusion matrix Γ: Controls magnitude short-term fluctuations noise affective system, characterizing individual’s reactivity sensitivity environmental perturbations. affectOU primarily serves demonstration packaged computational model, detailed Evers & Vanhasbroeck (2026), “Sharing Computational Models Reproducible User-Friendly Packages: Tutorial R” (forthcoming).

title: affectOU: Simulate Affect Dynamics with Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Processes — affectOU-package
text: Simulate multivariate Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) process model psychological affect dynamics. OU process continuous-time stochastic process simulated using Euler-Maruyama discretization. models affect self-regulating system three psychologically meaningful parameters: theta quantifies emotion regulation capacity (quickly affect returns baseline; lower values indicate emotional inertia rumination); mu represents person's affective set point typical mood state; gamma captures emotional reactivity (strongly affect responds ongoing fluctuations). OU can visualised using time trajectories, histograms, autocorrelation functions, phase plots. package supports fitting univariate OU models data, also provides theoretical properties OU. References: Uhlenbeck & Ornstein (1930) doi:10.1103/PhysRev.36.823 ; Oravecz, Tuerlinckx & Vandekerckhove (2009) doi:10.1007/s11336-008-9106-8 ; Kuppens, Allen & Sheeber (2010) doi:10.1177/0956797610372634 ; Kuppens, Oravecz & Tuerlinckx (2010) doi:10.1037/a0020962 .

code: affectOU(ndim = 1, theta = 0.5, mu = 0, sigma = 1, gamma = t(chol(sigma)))

title: Create Ornstein-Uhlenbeck affect model — affectOU
text: ndim Dimensionality affect process. Defaults 1 (univariate). needs specified inferred dimensions parameters. theta Attractor strength (rate return baseline). 1D: positive scalar. multidimensional: square matrix. mu Attractor location (baseline affect set point). 1D: scalar. multidimensional: vector. non-stationary models: \(\theta < 0\), process pushed away \(\mu\) rather toward ; \(\theta \approx 0\), \(\mu\) meaningful influence trajectory. sigma Noise covariance matrix (\(\Sigma = \Gamma\Gamma^\top\)). 1D: positive scalar (variance). multidimensional: positive semi-definite matrix. -diagonal elements represent correlated noise dimensions. recommended way specify noise structure. Specifying gamma sigma error. gamma Diffusion coefficient (multiplies \(dW(t)\) SDE). 1D: positive scalar. multidimensional: lower triangular matrix (Cholesky factor \(\Sigma\)). Specifying gamma sigma error. users prefer specifying sigma directly; gamma available advanced users want explicit control Cholesky factorisation.

title: Create Ornstein-Uhlenbeck affect model — affectOU
text: object class affectOU, representing univariate multivariate Ornstein–Uhlenbeck affect regulation model. object list following components: parameters named list model parameters: theta Numeric matrix. mu Numeric vector. gamma Numeric matrix. sigma Numeric matrix. stationary named list stationary distribution properties, precomputed construction: is_stable (logical), mean (numeric vector, always mu), sd (numeric vector NULL unstable), cov (matrix NULL), cor (matrix NULL), ndim (integer). ndim Integer.

title: Create Ornstein-Uhlenbeck affect model — affectOU
text: OU continuous-time stochastic differential equation model , multivariate variant, can written follows: $$d\mathbf{X}(t) = \mathbf{\Theta} (\mathbf{\mu} - \mathbf{X}(t))dt + \mathbf{\Gamma} d\mathbf{W}(t)$$ can simplified one-dimensional case : $$dX(t) = \theta (\mu - X(t))dt + \gamma dW(t)$$ : \(\mathbf{X}(t)\) represents affective state time \(t\); \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) (theta) represents drift matrix, governing rate affect returns baseline; \(\mathbf{\mu}\) (mu) represents location baseline attractor; \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\) (gamma) lower-triangular matrix governing size stochastic diffusion; \(\mathbf{W}(t)\) represents Wiener process, adding randomness system. Using matrix \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\), one can derive stationary covariance matrix \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) system using \(\mathbf{\Gamma}\) basis Cholesky decomposition solving Lyapunov equation, namely: $$\mathbf{\Gamma} \mathbf{\Gamma}^T = \mathbf{\Theta} \mathbf{\Sigma} - \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{\Theta}^T$$ multidimensional case, -diagonal elements drift matrix \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) determine temporal coupling different variables contained \(\mathbf{X}\), specifying variables co-evolve time.

title: Create Ornstein-Uhlenbeck affect model — affectOU
text: Oravecz, Z., Tuerlinckx, F., & Vandekerckhove, J. (2011). hierarchical latent stochastic differential equation model affective dynamics. Psychological Methods, 16(4), 468-490.

title: Create Ornstein-Uhlenbeck affect model — affectOU
code: # 1D model model_1d <- affectOU(theta = 0.5, mu = 0, sigma = 1) summary(model_1d) #>  #> ── 1D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Dynamics ── #>  #> Stable (node) #>  #> ── Stationary distribution ── #>  #> Mean: 0 #> SD: 1 coef(model_1d) #> $theta #> [1] 0.5 #>  #> $mu #> [1] 0 #>  #> $gamma #> [1] 1 #>  #> $sigma #> [1] 1 #>   # 2D model (uncoupled) model_2d <- affectOU(   theta = diag(c(0.5, 0.3)), mu = 0,   sigma = 1 ) summary(model_2d) #>  #> ── 2D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Dynamics ── #>  #> Stable (node) #>  #> ── Stationary distribution ── #>  #> Mean: [0, 0] #> SD: [1, 1.291] #>  #> ── Structure ── #>  #> Coupling: none #> Noise: independent  # Simulate trajectory sim <- simulate(model_2d, stop = 100, save_at = 0.1) plot(sim)   # 3D model (coupled) theta_3d <- matrix(c(   0.5, 0.1, 0,   0.1, 0.3, 0.05,   0, 0.05, 0.4 ), nrow = 3) model_3d <- affectOU(   theta = theta_3d,   mu = 0, sigma = 1 ) summary(model_3d) #>  #> ── 3D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Dynamics ── #>  #> Stable (node) #>  #> ── Stationary distribution ── #>  #> Mean: [0, 0, 0] #> SD: [1.036, 1.351, 1.131] #>  #> ── Structure ── #>  #> Coupling: Dim 1 → Dim 2 (+), Dim 2 → Dim 1 (+), Dim 2 → Dim 3 (+), Dim 3 → Dim #> 2 (+) #> Noise: independent  # Simulate trajectory sim_3d <- simulate(model_3d, stop = 100, save_at = 0.1) plot(sim_3d)

title: Extract model equations — equation.affectOU
code: # Plain text equation for 1D model model <- affectOU() equation(model) #> dX(t) = theta * (mu - X(t)) dt + gamma dW(t) #>  #> where: #>   theta = 0.5 #>   mu    = 0 #>   gamma = 1 #>   sigma = 1  # Inlined Latex equation for 2D model model <- affectOU(ndim = 2) equation(model, type = "latex", inline = TRUE) #> \begin{equation*} #> d\mathbf{X}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} #>   0.5 & 0 \\ #>   0 & 0.5 #> \end{pmatrix} \left( \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} - \mathbf{X}(t) \right) dt + \begin{pmatrix} #>   1 & 0 \\ #>   0 & 1 #> \end{pmatrix} \, d\mathbf{W}(t) #> \end{equation*}  # Expression output for 1D model model <- affectOU() equation(model, type = "expression") #> Equation: #> dX(t) == theta * (mu - X(t)) * dt + gamma * dW(t) #> theta = #> [1] 0.5 #> mu = #> [1] 0 #> gamma = #> [1] 1 #> sigma = #> [1] 1  # R code output for 1D model model <- affectOU() equation(model, type = "code") #> theta <- 0.5 #> mu <- 0 #> gamma <- 1 #> sigma <- 1 #>  #> dX <- theta * (mu - X) * dt + gamma * dW

title: Fit Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model to data — fit.affectOU
text: object object class affectOU. data Numeric vector observed affect values. times Numeric vector observation times. NULL, defaults equally spaced times: 0, 1, 2, … method Character string specifying estimation method. Currently "mle" (.e., maximum likelihood estimation) supported. start Optional named vector starting values parameters: theta, mu, gamma. NULL, reasonable defaults chosen based data. Specifically, theta initialized using empirical lag-1 autocorrelation, mu initialized sample mean, gamma initialized based stationary variance estimate. ... Additional arguments (unused)

text: object class fit_affectOU, containing: parameters Named list fitted parameter estimates. se Named list standard errors parameters. fitted_values Conditional means fitted model. residuals Vector residuals (data - fitted_values). log_likelihood Maximized log-likelihood value. rmse Root Mean Squared Error fitted values. convergence Optimizer convergence code. start List starting values used optimizer. nobs Number observations. data observed data used fitting. times observation times used. method Estimation method used (e.g., "mle"). model original affectOU model used fitting.

title: Fit Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model to data — fit.affectOU
code: model <- affectOU(theta = 0.5, mu = 0, gamma = 1) sim <- simulate(model, stop = 1000, dt = 0.01, save_at = 0.01) data <- as.data.frame(sim) fitted <- fit(model, data = data$value, times = data$time) print(fitted) #>  #> ── Fitted 1D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ────────────────────────────────────────── #> 100001 data points (dt ≈ 0.010) #> θ = 0.000, μ = 0.006, γ = 1.003 #> Log-likelihood: 88035.991 #> RMSE: 0.100 plot(fitted)

Faster decay means less predictability time. multivariate processes, diagonal panels show ACF dimension, -diagonal panels show cross-correlation function (CCF) dimension pairs, can non-zero even lag 0 dimensions correlated equilibrium.

title: Plot phase portrait — ou_plot_phase
text: diagonal panels plot \(X_i(t)\) \(X_i(t + \Delta t)\), showing relationship current next value dimension. system stable (\(\theta_{ii} > 0\)), conditional expectation : $$E[X_i(t + \Delta t) \mid X_i(t) = x] = \mu_i + (x - \mu_i)e^{-\theta_{ii} \Delta t}$$ line \((\mu_i, \mu_i)\) slope \(e^{-\theta_{ii} \Delta t} < 1\). slope less 1 reflects mean reversion: values \(\mu_i\) expected decrease; values \(\mu_i\) expected increase. star marks attractor point \((\mu_i, \mu_i)\). theoretical line drawn system stable.

title: Plot phase portrait — ou_plot_phase
text: -diagonal panels plot \(X_i(t)\) \(X_j(t)\) time point, showing joint distribution dimensions \(\) \(j\). system stable, conditional expectation based stationary covariance \(\Sigma_\infty\) : $$E[X_j \mid X_i = x] = \mu_j + \frac{\Sigma_{\infty,ij}}{\Sigma_{\infty,ii}}(x - \mu_i)$$ regression line \((\mu_i, \mu_j)\) slope determined stationary covariance structure. theoretical line drawn system stable.

title: Plot phase portrait — ou_plot_phase
code: model <- affectOU(ndim = 2) sim <- simulate(model, nsim = 3) ou_plot_phase(sim)   # Mean reversion visible in slope < 1 model <- affectOU(theta = 0.5, mu = 0, gamma = 1) sim <- simulate(model, stop = 100, dt = 0.01, save_at = 0.1) ou_plot_phase(sim) # slope = exp(-0.5 * 0.1) = 0.95

title: Simulation in Evers & Vanhasbroeck (2026) — simpaper
text: process defined stochastic differential equation: $$d\mathbf{X}_t = \mathbf{\Theta}(\mathbf{\mu} - \mathbf{X}_t)dt + \mathbf{\Gamma}^{1/2}d\mathbf{W}_t$$ : \(\mathbf{\Theta} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\) drift matrix \(\mathbf{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\) equilibrium mean vector \(\mathbf{\Gamma} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\) diffusion matrix \(\mathbf{W}_t\) two-dimensional Wiener process

title: Assess stability for Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model — stability.affectOU
text: Note although typically, system stable eigenvalues negative real parts, due parameterization OU process, stability determined positive real parts: $$dX(t) = \Theta(\mu - X(t))dt + \Gamma dW(t)$$ \(\Theta\) multiplied negative state variable, positive eigenvalues indicate deviations attractor decay time, leading stable system. Conversely, negative eigenvalues indicate deviations grow time, leading unstable system.

title: Assess stability for Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model — stability.affectOU
text: stationary distribution exists eigenvalues \(\Theta\) positive real parts. system-level property, per-dimension property: Positive diagonal elements (\(\Theta_{ii} > 0\)) alone guarantee stability. Strong -diagonal coupling can push eigenvalues toward zero negative real parts, destabilising system. Conversely, coupling dimensions can stabilise dimension whose diagonal element small even zero. dimension non-stationary isolation may become stationary embedded coupled system.

title: Assess stability for Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model — stability.affectOU
text: Complex eigenvalues (arising asymmetric coupling) produce oscillatory dynamics, dimensions cycle around . eigenvalues positive real parts, oscillations damped system still converges \(\mu\). eigenvalue non-positive real part, oscillations grow system diverges.

title: Assess stability for Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model — stability.affectOU
text: System-level dynamics classified using dynamical systems terms: node converges diverges without oscillation, spiral exhibits damped growing oscillations, saddle point mixed convergence/divergence across directions. Per-dimension dynamics describe behavior dimension isolation based diagonal element \(\Theta\) ("stable node" positive, "random walk" zero, "unstable node" negative). dimension classified "stable node" isolation may still belong unstable coupled system due coupling dimensions.

title: Assess stability for Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model — stability.affectOU
code: # 1D stable node model <- affectOU(theta = 0.5, mu = 0, gamma = 1) stability(model) #>  #> ── Stability analysis of 1D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ── #>  #> Stable (node). Deviations from the attractor decay exponentially. #>  #> Eigenvalues (all real): #> • λ1: 0.5  # 1D random walk (not stable) model_rw <- affectOU(theta = 0) stability(model_rw) #>  #> ── Stability analysis of 1D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ── #>  #> Not stable (random walk). No attractor; the process drifts freely. #>  #> Eigenvalues (all real): #> • λ1: 0  # Positive diagonals with oscillatory coupling: stable theta_osc <- matrix(c(0.5, -0.4, 0.4, 0.5), nrow = 2) stability(affectOU(theta = theta_osc, mu = 0, gamma = 1)) #>  #> ── Stability analysis of 2D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ── #>  #> Stable (spiral). The system spirals toward the attractor with damped #> oscillations. #>  #> Eigenvalues (complex): #> • λ1: 0.5 + 0.4i #> • λ2: 0.5 - 0.4i  # Strong coupling still stable if real parts stay positive theta_strong <- matrix(c(0.5, -1.5, 1.5, 0.5), nrow = 2) stability(affectOU(theta = theta_strong, mu = 0, gamma = 1)) #>  #> ── Stability analysis of 2D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ── #>  #> Stable (spiral). The system spirals toward the attractor with damped #> oscillations. #>  #> Eigenvalues (complex): #> • λ1: 0.5 + 1.5i #> • λ2: 0.5 - 1.5i  # All diagonals positive, but coupling destabilises the system theta_destab <- matrix(c(0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5), nrow = 2) stability(affectOU(theta = theta_destab, mu = 0, gamma = 1)) #>  #> ── Stability analysis of 2D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ── #>  #> Not stable (saddle point). Some directions converge while others diverge. #>  #> Eigenvalues (all real): #> • λ1: 1.5 #> • λ2: -0.5  # One negative diagonal element makes the system non-stationary theta_unstable <- matrix(c(0.5, 0, 0, -0.3), nrow = 2) stability(affectOU(theta = theta_unstable, mu = 0, gamma = 1)) #>  #> ── Stability analysis of 2D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ── #>  #> Not stable (saddle point). Some directions converge while others diverge. #>  #> • Dim. 1: stable node #> • Dim. 2: unstable node #>  #> Eigenvalues (all real): #>   • λ1: 0.5 #>   • λ2: -0.3

text: stationary distribution exists system stable (see stability()). non-stable systems, function returns is_stable = FALSE NULL distribution properties.

title: Compute stationary distribution for Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model — stationary.affectOU
text: \(\theta > 0\) (1D) eigenvalues \(\Theta\) positive real parts (multivariate), process converges stationary distribution: $$X_\infty \sim N\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{\gamma^2}{2\theta}\right)$$ stationary variance \(\gamma^2/(2\theta)\) depends \(\gamma\) \(\theta\). Different parameter combinations can produce long-run spread different dynamics (see examples).

title: Compute stationary distribution for Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model — stationary.affectOU
text: multivariate models, stationary covariance matrix \(\Sigma_\infty\) solves Lyapunov equation: $$\Theta \Sigma_\infty + \Sigma_\infty \Theta^\top = \Gamma \Gamma^T$$ -diagonal elements \(\Theta\) (coupling dimensions) can induce correlation equilibrium even noise independent.

title: Compute stationary distribution for Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model — stationary.affectOU
text: Key theoretical quantities 1D case: Stationary properties depend \(\gamma\) \(\theta\); temporal dynamics depend mainly \(\theta\). Two processes can share stationary distributions differ dynamics, vice versa.

title: Compute stationary distribution for Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model — stationary.affectOU
code: # 1D model model <- affectOU(theta = 0.5, mu = 0, gamma = 1) stationary(model) #>  #> ── Stationary distribution of 1D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ── #>  #> Mean: 0 #> SD: 1 #> 95% interval: [-2, 2]  # All components unclass(stationary(model)) #> $is_stable #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $mean #> [1] 0 #>  #> $sd #> [1] 1 #>  #> $cov #> NULL #>  #> $cor #> NULL #>  #> $ndim #> [1] 1 #>   # Different dynamics, same stationary distribution model_slow <- affectOU(theta = 0.5, mu = 0, gamma = 1) model_fast <- affectOU(theta = 2.0, mu = 0, gamma = 2) stationary(model_slow)$sd #> [1] 1 stationary(model_fast)$sd #> [1] 1  # Non-stable model model_rw <- affectOU(theta = 0) stationary(model_rw) #>  #> ── Stationary distribution of 1D Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model ── #>  #> Does not exist (system is not stable).  # 2D: coupling induces stationary correlation theta_2d <- matrix(c(0.5, 0.0, 0.3, 0.5), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE) model_2d <- affectOU(ndim = 2, theta = theta_2d, mu = 0, gamma = 1) stationary(model_2d)$cor # non-zero off-diagonal #>            [,1]       [,2] #> [1,]  1.0000000 -0.2761724 #> [2,] -0.2761724  1.0000000
